content.content:
1030	
1031	/// Commit app repo with `title` / `body` and the `ochid:` trailer
1032	/// pointing at `.claude`'s chid.
1033	fn stage_commit_app(
1034	    root: &Path,
1035	    state: &PushState,
1036	    args: &PushArgs,
1037	) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
1038	    let (title, body) = resolve_message(state, args)?;
1039	    let claude_chid = state
1040	        .claude_chid
1041	        .as_deref()
1042	        .ok_or("push commit-app: claude_chid not set (message stage didn't run)")?;
1043	    let body_with_trailer = format!("{body}\n\nochid: /.claude/{claude_chid}");
1044	    let app_arg = root.to_string_lossy();
1045	    if args.dry_run {
1046	        info!(
1047	            "push commit-app: [dry-run] would run jj commit -R {app_arg} -m \"{title}\" -m <body+ochid>"
1048	        );
1049	        return Ok(());
1050	    }
1051	    info!("push commit-app: jj commit -R {app_arg}");
1052	    run(
1053	        "jj",
1054	        &[
1055	            "commit",
1056	            "-R",
1057	            &app_arg,
1058	            "-m",
1059	            &title,
1060	            "-m",
1061	            &body_with_trailer,
1062	        ],
1063	        root,
1064	    )?;
1065	    Ok(())
1066	}
1067	
1068	/// Pull the title/body pair from CLI args or persisted state,
1069	/// preferring CLI args (override-on-resume case). Returns `Err`
1070	/// when neither source has them — which only happens if
1071	/// `stage_message` didn't run or was force-bypassed.
1072	fn resolve_message(
1073	    state: &PushState,
1074	    args: &PushArgs,
1075	) -> Result<(String, String), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
1076	    let title = args
1077	        .title
1078	        .clone()
1079	        .or_else(|| state.title.clone())
1080	        .ok_or("push: title missing — message stage didn't run")?;
1081	    let body = args
1082	        .body
1083	        .clone()
1084	        .or_else(|| state.body.clone())
1085	        .ok_or("push: body missing — message stage didn't run")?;
1086	    Ok((title, body))
1087	}
1088	
1089	/// Commit `.claude` with the same title/body and the ochid trailer
1090	/// pointing at the app commit's chid, or skip if `.claude` had no
1091	/// pending changes.
1092	fn stage_commit_claude(
1093	    root: &Path,
1094	    state: &PushState,
1095	    args: &PushArgs,
1096	) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
1097	    if !state.claude_had_changes.unwrap_or(false) {
1098	        info!("push commit-claude: skip (.claude had no pending changes)");
1099	        return Ok(());
1100	    }
1101	    let (title, body) = resolve_message(state, args)?;
1102	    let app_chid = state
1103	        .app_chid
1104	        .as_deref()

1251	fn verify_state_sanity(root: &Path, state: &PushState) -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
1252	    let Some(app_chid) = &state.app_chid else {
1253	        return Ok(()); // OK: fresh state, nothing to verify yet
1254	    };
1255	    let bookmark = &state.bookmark;
1256	    let root_str = root.to_string_lossy();
1257	    let cwd = Path::new(".");
1258	
1259	    // 1. app_chid still resolves.
1260	    run(
1261	        "jj",
1262	        &[
1263	            "log",
1264	            "-r",
1265	            app_chid,
1266	            "--no-graph",
1267	            "-T",
1268	            "\"x\"",
1269	            "-R",
1270	            &root_str,
1271	        ],
1272	        cwd,
1273	    )
1274	    .map_err(|_| -> Box<dyn std::error::Error> {
1275	        format!(
1276	            "state-sanity: app_chid '{app_chid}' has been abandoned or no longer resolves \
1277	             in the app repo. State is stale — run `vc-x1 push {bookmark} --restart` to clear."
1278	        )
1279	        .into()
1280	    })?;
1281	
1282	    // 2. bookmark at app_chid (after bookmark-both has run).
1283	    if matches!(state.stage, Stage::PushApp | Stage::FinalizeClaude) {
1284	        let bookmark_chid = run(
1285	            "jj",
1286	            &[
1287	                "log",
1288	                "-r",
1289	                bookmark,
1290	                "--no-graph",
1291	                "-T",
1292	                "change_id.short(12)",
1293	                "-R",
1294	                &root_str,
1295	            ],
1296	            cwd,
1297	        )?;
1298	        let bookmark_chid = bookmark_chid.trim();
1299	        if bookmark_chid != app_chid.as_str() {
1300	            return Err(format!(
1301	                "state-sanity: bookmark '{bookmark}' is at chid '{bookmark_chid}' but \
1302	                 state.app_chid is '{app_chid}'. State is stale — run \
1303	                 `vc-x1 push {bookmark} --restart` to clear."
1304	            )
1305	            .into());
1306	        }
1307	    }
1308	
1309	    // 3. claude_chid still resolves.
1310	    if let Some(claude_chid) = &state.claude_chid {
1311	        let claude = claude_path(root);
1312	        let claude_str = claude.to_string_lossy();
1313	        run(
1314	            "jj",
1315	            &[
1316	                "log",
1317	                "-r",
1318	                claude_chid,
1319	                "--no-graph",
1320	                "-T",
1321	                "\"x\"",
1322	                "-R",
1323	                &claude_str,
1324	            ],
1325	            cwd,
1326	        )
1327	        .map_err(|_| -> Box<dyn std::error::Error> {
1328	            format!(

content.text:
Validation summary:
- **Recipe (validated)**: `jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u` followed by no further moves. Squashes all intermediate substep commits up into `@`, leaves linear `<base> → @`, abandons empties, preserves `@`'s description (ready for `vc-x1 push` to overwrite with the close-out title/body).
- Tested on 3-substep ladder (`base..@-` = {s1, s2}) and 5-substep ladder (`base..@-` = {s1..s4}) — both produced clean linear history.
- The draft's `--from "base..@" --into base` recipe also works mechanically but leaves an empty `@` on top of `base`; subsequent `jj edit @-` makes `base` itself the working copy, which is awkward when `base` is meant to be the stable parent. Recipe-into-`@` is cleaner.
- The draft's `jj op restore` alternative is **not** a close-out tool — it undoes the substeps entirely and reverts WC to pre-substep state, which loses the work. Drop it from the formal protocol.

Substep 2 ended up as no-file-change validation work; I'll fold it into substep 3 (which encodes the findings into the rewritten doc) — the empty middle commit gets dropped on its own at close-out squash, but cleaner to not create one.

input.content:
# Substep protocol

When a single multi-step `X.Y.Z-N` cycle covers several
separable concerns and the per-step diff would be too large
to review in one pass, work each concern as its own substep
on its own jj `@`. The ladder collapses into a single
commit at close-out and ships through the normal
`vc-x1 push` flow.

This file is the protocol of record. CLAUDE.md points here.

## When to use

Use substeps when an `X.Y.Z-N` step splits naturally into
two or more units of work that:

- can be reviewed independently,
- want bisectable history during the cycle (so a regression
  surfaced by tests can be pinned to one substep),
- benefit from intermediate-test gates rather than one
  end-of-step gate.

Don't use substeps for trivial or single-concern steps. The
per-substep `cargo test --bins` cost is real; pay it only
when the review and bisection wins are real.

## Per-substep contract

For each substep:

1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
2. Do the substep's work on this `@`.
3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable.** Build and
   clippy alone are not enough — past experience
   (0.41.1-6.5) had a regression introduced in an early
   substep that didn't surface until a later substep ran
   the full suite, by which point bisection cost was
   higher than it needed to be.
4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — give the substep
   a working title and body. The title does **not** need a
   version suffix during the ladder; close-out collects
   every substep into one final commit which carries the
   `(X.Y.Z-N)` marker. Substep titles are scratch — they
   never reach the remote.

The ladder is local-only. Substep commits exist for review
and bisection during the cycle and disappear into the
close-out squash.

## Navigating the ladder

For revset basics (chid, cid, `@`, `@-`, `@+`, `..`, `::`,
prefix matching) see [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md).

Common moves while a ladder is active:

- `jj log -r 'all()' -R .` — see the whole ladder at a
  glance.
- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any commit in
  the ladder by chid prefix. Useful for bisection: re-run
  the failing test at each candidate without manual
  checkout juggling.
- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two substeps.
- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one substep's diff in
  isolation.

Substep work happens on whatever `@` is currently selected
— if you `jj edit` to an earlier substep and modify files,
that substep's commit is rewritten in place. Descendants
auto-rebase.

## Close-out

When all substeps are done and `cargo test --bins` is green
on the latest:

```
jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

Where `<base>` is the parent of the **first** substep —
typically the bookmark or commit the ladder branched from.
The `-u` flag (use destination message) tells jj to keep
`@`'s description and discard the source descriptions; the
intermediate titles served their purpose, and `vc-x1 push`
will overwrite `@`'s description anyway.

After squash:

- All substep diffs are folded into `@`.
- The intermediate substep commits become empty and are
  abandoned automatically.
- History is linear: `<base> → @`.

Then run `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` exactly as for any other
step. Push's `commit-app` stage finalizes `@` with the
close-out title/body and the proper `(X.Y.Z-N)` version
suffix.

### Validation status

The recipe was validated 2026-05-01 against scratch ladders
built by [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh):

- 3-substep ladder (`base..@-` = {s1, s2}, `@ = s3`):
  squash produces `base → @` linear; intermediate commits
  abandoned; `@` retains its description.
- 5-substep ladder (`base..@-` = {s1..s4}, `@ = s5`): same
  outcome at scale.

The recipe is N-agnostic for N ≥ 2 substeps. For N = 1 the
close-out squash is a no-op (`base..@-` is empty when `@-`
is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.

## Recovery

If a substep goes sideways and you want to back out without
losing prior substeps:

- **Discard the current substep's work.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
  drops it and gives you a fresh empty `@` on the same
  parent. Earlier substeps are untouched.
- **Edit an earlier substep.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-substep-chid>`
  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows the
  op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts the repo
  to that point. This is a full undo — it removes *all*
  substep work after the chosen op. Use only when you want
  to start over. **Not** a close-out recipe; the squash
  above is.

## Worked example

A ladder of 3 substeps starting from bookmark `main` at
commit `abc1234`:

```
jj new -R .                                # substep 1
... edit files, cargo test --bins ...
jj describe -m "substep 1: ..." -R .

jj new -R .                                # substep 2
... edit files, cargo test --bins ...
jj describe -m "substep 2: ..." -R .

jj new -R .                                # substep 3
... edit files, cargo test --bins ...
jj describe -m "substep 3: ..." -R .
```

`jj log -r 'all()'` now shows:

```
@  yyy ... substep 3: ...
○  xxx ... substep 2: ...
○  www ... substep 1: ...
○  abc1234 main | <previous step's commit>
```

Close out:

```
jj squash --from "main..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

Result:

```
@  yyy ... substep 3: ...   (now contains all substep diffs)
○  abc1234 main | <previous step's commit>
```

Then `vc-x1 push main --title "..." --body "..."` finalizes
the cycle.

# References

- [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md) — revset primitives
  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
  for squash-recipe experiments.
- 0.41.1-6.5 cycle — first multi-substep usage. The
  per-substep `cargo test --bins` gate originated there
  after a regression introduced in an early substep wasn't
  caught until a later substep ran the full suite.

+# Substep protocol
-The 0.41.1-6.5 cycle was the first use of the per-substep `@`
-protocol. It worked well for review/bisection but exposed a
-contract mismatch with `vc-x1 push`. Capture for CLAUDE.md
-before the next multi-substep cycle.
+When a single multi-step `X.Y.Z-N` cycle covers several
+separable concerns and the per-step diff would be too large
+to review in one pass, work each concern as its own substep
+on its own jj `@`. The ladder collapses into a single
+commit at close-out and ships through the normal
+`vc-x1 push` flow.
-**Draft text — proposed addition under
-`## Pre-commit Requirements > ### Versioning`:**
+This file is the protocol of record. CLAUDE.md points here.
-> #### Substep protocol (within a multi-step `X.Y.Z-N`)
-> For multi-step cycles where a single `X.Y.Z-N` covers several
-> separable concerns, work each substep on its own `@`:
-> jj new -R .   # start substep (1)
-> ... do substep (1) work + cargo test --bins ...
-> jj new -R .   # start substep (2)
-> ... do substep (2) work + cargo test --bins ...
-> At close-out, fold the substep `@`s into one commit and proceed
-> with the normal `vc-x1 push` flow.
-> **Wins observed (0.41.1-6.5):** per-substep diffs stay clean
-> for review; bisection is a single `jj edit` jump per candidate;
-> intermediate failure can be its own substep that's visible in
-> the substep ladder.
-> **Hard rule — `cargo test --bins` per substep.** Build + clippy
-> between substeps isn't enough. A regression introduced in (1)
-> of -6.5 wasn't caught until (4) ran the full test suite.
-> **Close-out — squash + edit.** The bot thinks the cleanest way
-> to reconcile per-substep `@`s with `vc-x1 push`'s contract
-> (work-uncommitted-in-`@`) is:
-> jj squash --from "@<base>..@" --into @<base> -R .
-> jj edit @- -R .         # move @ onto the squashed commit
-> vc-x1 push <bookmark> --yes --title "..." --body "..."
-> `@<base>` is `@---` for a 3-substep ladder, `@----` for 4, etc.
-> The bot thinks `jj op restore <op-before-first-substep>` is an
-> alternative that collapses all substep work back into `@`
-> uncommitted, but neither path has been re-validated since the
-> recovery in -6.5; treat as a working hypothesis until the next
-> multi-substep cycle exercises it.
+## When to use
-**Notes for tomorrow's work:**
+Use substeps when an `X.Y.Z-N` step splits naturally into
+two or more units of work that:
-- This lands as its own subcycle (suggest 0.41.1-6.6.0 or whatever
-  the next `-N` slot is). CLAUDE.md-only edit, no code changes.
-- Verify the squash + edit recipe above by trying it cleanly on a
-  scratch `jj new`/`jj new` chain before relying on it for a real
-  cycle. If `jj op restore` is preferred, validate that path too.
-- Once verified, drop the "The bot thinks ..." markers and replace
-  with a confirmed recipe. Note in chores which path was adopted.
+- can be reviewed independently,
+- want bisectable history during the cycle (so a regression
+  surfaced by tests can be pinned to one substep),
+- benefit from intermediate-test gates rather than one
+  end-of-step gate.
-### Decisions made during design
+Don't use substeps for trivial or single-concern steps. The
+per-substep `cargo test --bins` cost is real; pay it only
+when the review and bisection wins are real.
+## Per-substep contract
+For each substep:
+1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
+2. Do the substep's work on this `@`.
+3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable.** Build and
+   clippy alone are not enough — past experience
+   (0.41.1-6.5) had a regression introduced in an early
+   substep that didn't surface until a later substep ran
+   the full suite, by which point bisection cost was
+   higher than it needed to be.
+4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — give the substep
+   a working title and body. The title does **not** need a
+   version suffix during the ladder; close-out collects
+   every substep into one final commit which carries the
+   `(X.Y.Z-N)` marker. Substep titles are scratch — they
+   never reach the remote.
+The ladder is local-only. Substep commits exist for review
+and bisection during the cycle and disappear into the
+close-out squash.
+## Navigating the ladder
+For revset basics (chid, cid, `@`, `@-`, `@+`, `..`, `::`,
+prefix matching) see [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md).
+Common moves while a ladder is active:
+- `jj log -r 'all()' -R .` — see the whole ladder at a
+  glance.
+- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any commit in
+  the ladder by chid prefix. Useful for bisection: re-run
+  the failing test at each candidate without manual
+  checkout juggling.
+- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two substeps.
+- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one substep's diff in
+  isolation.
+Substep work happens on whatever `@` is currently selected
+— if you `jj edit` to an earlier substep and modify files,
+that substep's commit is rewritten in place. Descendants
+auto-rebase.
+## Close-out
+When all substeps are done and `cargo test --bins` is green
+on the latest:
+jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
+Where `<base>` is the parent of the **first** substep —
+typically the bookmark or commit the ladder branched from.
+The `-u` flag (use destination message) tells jj to keep
+`@`'s description and discard the source descriptions; the
+intermediate titles served their purpose, and `vc-x1 push`
+will overwrite `@`'s description anyway.
+After squash:
+- All substep diffs are folded into `@`.
+- The intermediate substep commits become empty and are
+  abandoned automatically.
+- History is linear: `<base> → @`.
+Then run `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` exactly as for any other
+step. Push's `commit-app` stage finalizes `@` with the
+close-out title/body and the proper `(X.Y.Z-N)` version
+suffix.
+### Validation status
+The recipe was validated 2026-05-01 against scratch ladders
+built by [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh):
+- 3-substep ladder (`base..@-` = {s1, s2}, `@ = s3`):
+  squash produces `base → @` linear; intermediate commits
+  abandoned; `@` retains its description.
+- 5-substep ladder (`base..@-` = {s1..s4}, `@ = s5`): same
+  outcome at scale.
+The recipe is N-agnostic for N ≥ 2 substeps. For N = 1 the
+close-out squash is a no-op (`base..@-` is empty when `@-`
+is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.
+## Recovery
+If a substep goes sideways and you want to back out without
+losing prior substeps:
+- **Discard the current substep's work.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
+  drops it and gives you a fresh empty `@` on the same
+  parent. Earlier substeps are untouched.
+- **Edit an earlier substep.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
+  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-substep-chid>`
+  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
+- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows the
+  op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts the repo
+  to that point. This is a full undo — it removes *all*
+  substep work after the chosen op. Use only when you want
+  to start over. **Not** a close-out recipe; the squash
+  above is.
+## Worked example
+A ladder of 3 substeps starting from bookmark `main` at
+commit `abc1234`:
+jj new -R .                                # substep 1
+... edit files, cargo test --bins ...
+jj describe -m "substep 1: ..." -R .
+jj new -R .                                # substep 2
+... edit files, cargo test --bins ...
+jj describe -m "substep 2: ..." -R .
+jj new -R .                                # substep 3
+... edit files, cargo test --bins ...
+jj describe -m "substep 3: ..." -R .
+`jj log -r 'all()'` now shows:
+@  yyy ... substep 3: ...
+○  xxx ... substep 2: ...
+○  www ... substep 1: ...
+○  abc1234 main | <previous step's commit>
+Close out:
+jj squash --from "main..@-" --into @ -u -R .
+Result:
+@  yyy ... substep 3: ...   (now contains all substep diffs)
+○  abc1234 main | <previous step's commit>
+Then `vc-x1 push main --title "..." --body "..."` finalizes
+the cycle.
+# References
+- [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md) — revset primitives
+  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
+- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
+  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
+  for squash-recipe experiments.
+- 0.41.1-6.5 cycle — first multi-substep usage. The
+  per-substep `cargo test --bins` gate originated there
+  after a regression introduced in an early substep wasn't
+  caught until a later substep ran the full suite.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
### Substep protocol — capture for CLAUDE.md (planned for next subcycle)

The 0.41.1-6.5 cycle was the first use of the per-substep `@`
protocol. It worked well for review/bisection but exposed a
contract mismatch with `vc-x1 push`. Capture for CLAUDE.md
before the next multi-substep cycle.

**Draft text — proposed addition under
`## Pre-commit Requirements > ### Versioning`:**

> #### Substep protocol (within a multi-step `X.Y.Z-N`)
>
> For multi-step cycles where a single `X.Y.Z-N` covers several
> separable concerns, work each substep on its own `@`:
>
> ```
> jj new -R .   # start substep (1)
> ... do substep (1) work + cargo test --bins ...
> jj new -R .   # start substep (2)
> ... do substep (2) work + cargo test --bins ...
> ... etc ...
> ```
>
> At close-out, fold the substep `@`s into one commit and proceed
> with the normal `vc-x1 push` flow.
>
> **Wins observed (0.41.1-6.5):** per-substep diffs stay clean
> for review; bisection is a single `jj edit` jump per candidate;
> intermediate failure can be its own substep that's visible in
> the substep ladder.
>
> **Hard rule — `cargo test --bins` per substep.** Build + clippy
> between substeps isn't enough. A regression introduced in (1)
> of -6.5 wasn't caught until (4) ran the full test suite.
>
> **Close-out — squash + edit.** The bot thinks the cleanest way
> to reconcile per-substep `@`s with `vc-x1 push`'s contract
> (work-uncommitted-in-`@`) is:
>
> ```
> jj squash --from "@<base>..@" --into @<base> -R .
> jj edit @- -R .         # move @ onto the squashed commit
> vc-x1 push <bookmark> --yes --title "..." --body "..."
> ```
>
> `@<base>` is `@---` for a 3-substep ladder, `@----` for 4, etc.
> The bot thinks `jj op restore <op-before-first-substep>` is an
> alternative that collapses all substep work back into `@`
> uncommitted, but neither path has been re-validated since the
> recovery in -6.5; treat as a working hypothesis until the next
> multi-substep cycle exercises it.

**Notes for tomorrow's work:**

- This lands as its own subcycle (suggest 0.41.1-6.6.0 or whatever
  the next `-N` slot is). CLAUDE.md-only edit, no code changes.
- Verify the squash + edit recipe above by trying it cleanly on a
  scratch `jj new`/`jj new` chain before relying on it for a real
  cycle. If `jj op restore` is preferred, validate that path too.
- Once verified, drop the "The bot thinks ..." markers and replace
  with a confirmed recipe. Note in chores which path was adopted.

### Decisions made during design

aiTitle: Review jj-revsets notes and document substep protocol

content.subject: Substep 1: jj-revsets.md review fixes
content.description: Apply the typo/structure fixes called out during initial review: usde→used, missing quote on root(), double-space in chid line, incomplete sentence on line 33, operator-pair interpretations (@..  @::  ..@  ::@), prefix-must-be-unambiguous rule, blank @+++/@--- output note. Run cargo test --bins after.

content.subject: Substep 2: validate close-out squash recipes
content.description: Use /tmp/substep-test (rerun substep-test.sh fresh) to actually exercise the candidate squash recipes from substep-protocol.md and sc.txt. Goal: identify the recipe that cleanly collapses an N-substep ladder into a single commit + working copy in the right place, suitable for vc-x1 push.

content.subject: Substep 3: rewrite substep-protocol.md as formal initial protocol
content.description: Drop "working hypothesis" hedges where validation confirms; restructure as a clean standalone doc — purpose, when-to-use, per-substep contract (cargo test --bins), navigation, close-out recipe, recovery. Cross-link to jj-revsets.md for the revset primitives.

content.subject: Substep 4: integrate into CLAUDE.md and tighten organization
content.description: Add a substep-protocol pointer in CLAUDE.md (under Versioning, per the original draft framing). While there, look for low-hanging organization wins — duplications, headers that could be merged, sections that could move to notes/. Conservative scope: only changes that clearly help readability.

input.command:
jj describe -m "docs: substep-protocol.md formal protocol" -m "Replaces the prior \"draft text\" framing with a standalone
formal protocol document. Codifies what was learned in
substep 2's close-out squash validation against
substep-test.sh-built ladders.

Structure:
- Purpose / when to use.
- Per-substep contract (jj new → work → cargo test --bins
  → jj describe). Substep titles do NOT carry version
  suffixes; the close-out commit does.
- Navigation (cross-link to jj-revsets.md for revset
  primitives).
- Close-out recipe — validated:
    jj squash --from \"<base>..@-\" --into @ -u -R .
  Rationale for -u (use destination message), N-agnostic
  for N >= 2, no-op for N = 1.
- Recovery: abandon current substep, edit earlier substep,
  full-undo via jj op restore. Calls out that op restore
  is NOT a close-out tool (the draft had floated it as
  alternative; validation showed it discards substep work).
- Worked example end-to-end.

Validation evidence baked into the recipe section: 3-substep
and 5-substep ladders both produce linear base→@ history
with intermediate empties auto-abandoned and @'s
description preserved (which vc-x1 push then overwrites).

cargo test --bins: 265 passed." -R .

content.content:
1	# CLAUDE.md - Bot Instructions
2	
3	## Project Structure
4	
5	This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:
6	
7	1. **App repo** (`/` — project root): Contains the application source code.
8	2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.
9	
10	Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.
11	
12	## Repo Paths (relative from project root)
13	
14	- App repo: `.` (project root)
15	- Bot session repo: `.claude`
16	  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)
17	
18	## Working Directory
19	
20	Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
21	to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
22	discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
23	command failures downstream.
24	
25	When invoking shell commands, prefer the shortest unambiguous path —
26	usually relative to cwd (`ls notes/`, not
27	`ls /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/notes/`). Long absolute paths
28	clutter the transcript without adding information. Out-of-workspace
29	paths (`/tmp/...`, `~/.config/...`) stay absolute. Read/Edit/Write
30	tool args also stay absolute — that's a tool-boundary constraint,
31	not a stylistic choice.
32	
33	## Memory
34	
35	Do not use the bot's per-project memory directory
36	(`~/.claude/projects/<path>/memory/`). In a dual-repo setup with
37	CLAUDE.md it provides no capability CLAUDE.md doesn't already cover,
38	and it loses on discoverability:
39	
40	- **CLAUDE.md** — at the repo root, a well-known location for bot
41	  instructions, committed, reviewable, visible to every collaborator
42	  (human or bot).
43	- **Memory directory** — hidden under the user's home, tied to one
44	  machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed.
45	
46	Easy for everyone to find beats convenient for the bot alone. Put
47	durable context in CLAUDE.md (or committed `notes/`) instead.
48	
49	## Speculation marker
50	
51	Durable text the bot writes — CLAUDE.md, `notes/`, commit bodies,
52	chores sections — should stick to observations and direct descriptions
53	of the code or data. If a mechanism, hypothesis, or causal claim
54	enters the text, prefix it with "The bot thinks ..." so a reader can
55	tell the measured from the inferred.
56	
57	**Why:** unmarked speculation reads like evidence, and a future reader
58	(or the bot on a later session) can pick it up as a known fact when
59	it's not. Measured / inferred is a distinction worth keeping visible
60	in the written record.
61	
62	**How to apply:** observations and factual descriptions need no
63	marker. Prefix with "The bot thinks ..." (or a close variant like
64	"The bot's guess is ...") when the claim is a mechanism ("X wins
65	because Y caches better"), a cause ("the drift was due to thermal
66	state"), a prediction ("this should scale linearly"), or any
67	reasoning not directly supported by the data on hand.
68	
69	## Committing
70	
71	Use `-R` (`--repository`) at the end to target the correct repo. Use
72	relative paths to reduce noise. Putting `-R` last keeps the verb/action
73	visible at the start of the command.
74	
75	### App repo
76	```
77	jj commit -m \
78	"title" \
79	-m "body
80	
81	ochid: /.claude/<changeID>" \
82	-R .
83	```
84	
85	### Bot session repo
86	```
87	jj commit -m \
88	"title" \
89	-m "body
90	
91	ochid: /<changeID>" \
92	-R .claude
93	```
94	
95	## jj Basics
96	
97	- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
98	- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
99	- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
100	- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
101	- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` — push a bookmark (no
102	  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
103	- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
104	  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
105	- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
106	  session because session data updates continuously.
107	
108	## Commit Message Style
109	
110	Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with
111	a version suffix:
112	
113	```
114	<type>: <short description> (<version>)
115	```
116	
117	- **Title**: target ~50 chars, short summary of *what* changed.
118	  Include the version. Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`,
119	  `test`, `docs`, `chore`.
120	- **App-repo body**: short intro paragraph (1–3 sentences), then a
121	  terse bullet list. Each bullet corresponds one-to-one with the
122	  edits structure already documented in `notes/chores-*.md` for
123	  this step — just the file and a one-line gist (e.g.
124	  `README.md: new Overview intro`). Do *not* restate the detail
125	  that lives in chores; the commit body is a scan-able index, not
126	  a duplicate. The chores section is the source of truth.
127	- **Session-repo body**: terse intro + a few session-activity
128	  bullets. Doesn't need to mirror chores since it describes
129	  in-session work, not code changes.
130	- Examples:
131	  - `feat: add fix-ochid subcommand (0.22.0)`
132	  - `fix: fix-ochid prefix bug (0.22.1)`
133	  - `refactor: deduplicate common CLI flags (0.21.1)`
134	
135	## Pre-commit Requirements
136	
137	### User approval
138	
139	Never execute commit, squash, push, or finalize commands without the
140	user's explicit approval. Present changes for review first; only run
141	them after the user confirms. This applies to late changes too —
142	pause for review before squashing into an existing commit.
143	
144	### Review before proposing the commit block
145	
146	After finishing a unit of work, **summarize what changed and stop
147	there**. Do not pre-emptively lay out the Checkpoint-1 commit
148	commands. Wait for the user to signal review is complete before
149	proposing the commit block. Changes during review are the norm,
150	not the exception; proposing commit text too early creates noise
151	and signals that I consider the work done when it usually isn't.
152	
153	This applies per-step in a multi-step flow too — each step gets a
154	review pause before its commit block appears.
155	
156	Signals that review is complete include explicit approval ("let's
157	commit", "looks good, commit it") **and any directive to start the
158	next step** ("do step 4", "next", "go N+1"). In that case the
159	previous step must be committed first — always commit the current
160	step before starting the next; don't ask.
161	
162	### Notes references
163	
164	Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
165	See [Todo format](notes/README.md#todo-format) for details.
166	
167	### Markdown anchor links
168	
169	GitHub anchor algorithm: lowercase, strip non-alphanumeric
170	characters in place, map remaining spaces to hyphens 1-for-1. Do
171	**not** collapse adjacent whitespace — so `a + b` → `a--b` (spaces
172	on both sides of `+`), but `a: b` → `a-b` (only trailing space on
173	`:`). General markdown reference:
174	[markdownguide.org](https://www.markdownguide.org). GitHub
175	publishes no official spec for auto-generated anchors; the
176	de-facto reference implementation is
177	[github-slugger](https://github.com/Flet/github-slugger).
178	
179	### Versioning
180	
181	Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based
182	on scope:
183	
184	- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump
185	  directly to `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
186	- **Multi-step** (for exploratory/large changes): bump to `X.Y.Z-0`,
187	  implement across multiple commits incrementing the numeric
188	  suffix. The final commit drops the suffix.
189	
190	The plan should recommend one approach and get user approval before
191	starting.
192	
193	For multi-step:
194	
195	1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-0` with the plan and commit as a chore
196	   marker.
197	2. Implement in one or more `X.Y.Z-N` commits (increment N as
198	   needed).
199	3. Final commit bumps to `X.Y.Z` (no suffix), updates
200	   `notes/todo.md` and `notes/chores-*.md` — this is the "done"
201	   marker.
202	
203	Multi-step cycles surface the ladder at the top of
204	`notes/todo.md > ## In Progress` as a bullet list with `(done)` /
205	`(current)` markers — see the file's intro paragraph for the
206	format. Update the markers as each step ships so the In Progress
207	view stays current at a glance.
208	
209	**Why numeric suffixes (`-0`, `-1`, …) rather than `-devN`:**
210	semver pre-release identifiers may consist of a single numeric
211	component, and they compare numerically per spec. So
212	`X.Y.Z-1 < X.Y.Z-2 < … < X.Y.Z` correctly orders the dev ladder
213	below the done marker. Cargo accepts this form. The `-dev` prefix
214	adds no information the git log doesn't already convey and
215	doubles typing per commit.
216	
217	The final release commit (no suffix) signals completion rather than
218	amending prior commits. This keeps history readable and makes it easy
219	to see which commits were exploratory vs final.
220	
221	### Chores section headers
222	
223	Chores section headers use trailing version format:
224	
225	```
226	## Description (X.Y.Z)
227	```
228	
229	Example: `` ## Add `fn claude-symlink` (0.27.0) ``
230	
231	### Pre-commit checklist
232	
233	Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:
234	
235	1. `cargo fmt`
236	2. `cargo clippy`
237	3. `cargo test`
238	4. `cargo install --path . --locked` (if applicable) — `--locked`
239	   is required: without it, `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock`
240	   and re-resolves from scratch, which can pick incompatible
241	   versions even when `cargo build` / `cargo test` succeed.
242	5. Retest after install
243	6. Update `notes/todo.md` — add to `## Done` if completing a task
244	7. Update `notes/chores-*.md` — add a subsection describing the change
245	8. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new flags,
246	   new subcommands, changed behavior)
247	
248	## Code Conventions
249	
250	### Doc comments on every file, function, and method
251	
252	Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
253	function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
254	one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
255	comment exists, not that it be long.
256	
257	This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
258	default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
259	module / item surface are expected; inline explanatory comments
260	inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
261	non-obvious WHY.
262	
263	**Shape:** short intro (1–3 sentences, shorter is better), then
264	a `-` bullet list for any structured details. Avoid long prose
265	paragraphs — they read as a wall of text and hide the structure
266	that bullets make scannable. Same shape applies to:
267	
268	- Module / function / struct / field doc comments in `.rs` files.
269	- Chore descriptions in `notes/chores-NN.md`.
270	- Todo entries in `notes/todo.md` (when an item needs more than
271	  one line of detail; pure one-liners are still fine).
272	
273	**Clap-derive args:** doc comments on `#[arg(...)]` fields drive
274	`--help` output. Clap reflows by default and collapses bullets
275	into running prose. Add `#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, ...)]` on
276	any field whose doc comment uses bullets so each `- …` lands on
277	its own line in the rendered help.
278	
279	### `// OK: …` comments on `unwrap*` calls (Rust)
280	
281	Non-test code that calls `.unwrap()`, `.unwrap_or(…)`,
282	`.unwrap_or_default()`, or `.unwrap_or_else(…)` must have a trailing
283	`// OK: …` comment that justifies why the call is acceptable.
284	
285	- `// OK: <specific reason>` — document the real precondition,
286	  invariant, or domain reason. Preferred whenever the reason isn't
287	  self-evident.
288	- `// OK: obvious` — the default is self-evident from context (e.g.
289	  `desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("")` — empty desc → empty title).
290	
291	Bare `// OK` is not used (reads like a truncated comment).
292	Abbreviations (e.g. `SE`) are not used because they require a decoder
293	ring for readers seeing the code out of context.
294	
295	For provably-unreachable `.unwrap()` calls, also prefix with
296	`#[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]` so the site stays silent if we enable
297	the project-wide `clippy::unwrap_used` lint later.
298	
299	```rust
300	let max = stderr_level.unwrap_or(LevelFilter::Info); // OK: default verbosity when -v/-vv absent
301	let first_line = desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("");  // OK: obvious
302	
303	match matches.len() {
304	    1 => {
305	        #[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]
306	        // OK: `1 =>` arm guarantees matches.len() == 1
307	        Ok(TitleMatch::One(matches.into_iter().next().unwrap()))
308	    }
309	    // ...
310	}
311	```
312	
313	Tests (`#[cfg(test)]`) are exempt — panicking on setup failure is the
314	correct test behavior.
315	
316	### Ask for clarification on ambiguous input
317	
318	When user input is ambiguous or missing necessary detail, stop and
319	ask a specific question. Do not proceed on a guess and hope the
320	result lands right — a clarifying question costs a few seconds;
321	redoing misaligned work costs much more.
322	
323	### Recognize when stuck
324	
325	If a simple task has eaten 5+ minutes of thinking or back-and-forth
326	without progress, stop. Summarize what's blocking — unclear
327	requirements, unfamiliar API surface, conflicting signals — and ask.
328	Continued flailing produces worse outcomes than a direct "I'm stuck
329	on X."
330	
331	## ochid Trailers
332	
333	Every commit body must include an `ochid:` trailer pointing to the
334	counterpart commit in the other repo. The value is a workspace-root-relative
335	path followed by the changeID:
336	
337	- App repo commits point to `.claude`: `ochid: /.claude/<changeID>`
338	- Bot session commits point to app repo: `ochid: /<changeID>`
339	
340	Use `vc-x1 chid -R .,.claude -L` to get both changeIDs (first line
341	is app repo, second is `.claude`).
342	
343	## Commit-Push-Finalize Flow
344	
345	**Use `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` — it wraps the full flow in one
346	command with two interactive approval gates.** Push runs
347	preflight (sync + fmt + clippy + test), prompts for review,
348	composes the commit message (via `--title`/`--body` or `$EDITOR`),
349	prompts for message approval, then commits both repos → advances
350	bookmarks → pushes app → finalizes `.claude`. Failures inside the
351	local mutation window roll both repos back via `jj op restore`;
352	after `push-app` succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
353	recovery is forward-only.
354	
355	**Run this flow after every step** — not only at session end.
356	Single-step and multi-step changes are of equal importance: a
357	single-step change is one `push` invocation; a multi-step change
358	is one `push` per `X.Y.Z-N` commit plus one for the final release
359	commit. Each step gets its own commit, its own push, and its own
360	finalize — so dev markers land on the remote and in `.claude` as
361	they happen rather than being batched until the end.
362	
363	### Run `vc-x1 push`
364	
365	```
366	vc-x1 push main                                      # interactive (review + $EDITOR)
367	vc-x1 push main --title "..." --body "..."           # flags skip $EDITOR
368	vc-x1 push main --yes --title "..." --body "..."     # full non-interactive
369	vc-x1 push main --dry-run                            # preview (no side effects)
370	vc-x1 push main --from commit-app                    # resume from specific stage
371	vc-x1 push --status                                  # show saved state
372	vc-x1 push main --restart                            # clear saved state; start fresh
373	```
374	
375	The two approval gates are surfaced by push itself:
376	
377	1. **Review** — push prints `jj diff --stat` for both repos and
378	   prompts `[y/N]`. Approve = "the work is done right".
379	2. **Message** — push either uses `--title`/`--body` (non-editor
380	   path) or opens `$EDITOR` on a template. Approve = "the message
381	   reads right".
382	
383	Both titles and bodies are the **same** across the two commits;
384	only the `ochid:` trailer differs per repo. Push collects the
385	pre-commit chids internally so you don't hand-manage them.
386	
387	For the full flag list and stage machine, see `vc-x1 push --help`
388	and `notes/chores-05.md > Add push subcommand (0.37.0)`.
389	
390	### Bot communication during the flow
391	
392	When applying the flow on the user's behalf, use plain prose at
393	each gate — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal", "Checkpoint N",
394	etc.):
395	
396	1. **After completing the work** — summarize what changed
397	   (file-by-file or feature-by-feature, terse) and end with
398	   something like:
399	
400	   > Work complete. Please review. On approval I'll prep the
401	   > commit title and body for a second approval before pushing.
402	
403	2. **After review approval** — present the proposed `$TITLE` and
404	   `$BODY` explicitly, then ask permission to run `vc-x1 push`.
405	   Do **not** spell out the full
406	   `vc-x1 push <bookmark> --yes --title "$TITLE" --body "$BODY"`
407	   invocation by default — it's mechanical and obvious from the
408	   title/body. Show it only on explicit request (verbose mode /
409	   debugging).
410	
411	3. **After execution approval** — run the push command. `push`
412	   handles commit + bookmark + push + finalize internally;
413	   nothing should be output after the push command (finalize is
414	   detached and absolute-last; see "After finalize: stop and
415	   wait" below).
416	
417	### Pre-step: `vc-x1 sync` (still useful)
418	
419	Push's preflight runs `vc-x1 sync --check` as its first step. Check
420	mode verifies divergence without applying — if anything is `behind`
421	or `diverged`, preflight errors and you resolve explicitly with
422	`vc-x1 sync --no-check` before re-running push. This keeps the
423	two approval gates (review, message) authoritative; push never
424	performs an unsupervised rebase.
425	
426	Running sync manually before you *start* editing is still cheap
427	(one line when clean) and surfaces remote changes earlier:
428	
429	```
430	vc-x1 sync --check                    # dual-repo workspace, verify
431	vc-x1 sync --no-check                 # apply (rebase / fast-forward)
432	vc-x1 sync --check -R .               # single-repo project
433	vc-x1 sync --check --quiet            # silent; exit code signals result
434	```
435	
436	Bots and scripts must pass `--check` or `--no-check` explicitly —
437	defaults can shift, explicit flags lock in the contract. Interactive
438	use can take the default (which is `--check`).
439	
440	Output shape:
441	
442	- **Clean**: one line — `sync: N repos, all bookmarks up-to-date`.
443	  Note: scope is bookmark-vs-remote tracking, not working-copy
444	  cleanliness — `@` may have uncommitted changes; sync doesn't
445	  speak to that. Proceed.
446	- **Action needed** (`behind` / `diverged`) under `--check`:
447	  per-repo fetch + state lines, then a fatal
448	  `sync: N repos need action — resolve with vc-x1 sync --no-check
449	  and re-run`. Inspect, run `vc-x1 sync --no-check`, then proceed.
450	- **`--quiet`**: no output; exit code is the only signal.
451	
452	### After finalize: stop and wait
453	
454	After `vc-x1 finalize` is launched — **whether mid-session per-step
455	or at session end** — you **MUST NEVER** proceed to a next step, edit
456	files, run tools, or emit any text (prose, recaps, acknowledgements),
457	until the user explicitly directs you to continue. Treat finalize as
458	a hard stop for the whole turn. Any final words (e.g. "next is ...")
459	must be said in the approval prompt *before* executing finalize; the
460	finalize `Bash` call is the last thing in the turn and nothing
461	follows it.
462	
463	This holds even when the next step seems obvious (e.g. "next is
464	N+1" or "now I should bump the version and commit the release").
465	Wait. The user controls cadence — every push+finalize is a checkpoint
466	they may want to inspect, think about, hand off, or take a break at.
467	Auto-proceeding bypasses that checkpoint and produces unwanted writes
468	between finalize and the next explicit instruction.
469	
470	Exceptions to this rule may emerge later but are not authorized at
471	this stage. Until told otherwise, treat as absolute.
472	
473	### Late changes after push
474	
475	If the app repo needs a tweak after `push-app` succeeded (e.g.
476	updating CLAUDE.md or memory), the commit is immutable. Use
477	`--ignore-immutable` to squash the changes into `@-` — the
478	bookmark moves with the rewritten commit — then re-push:
479	
480	```
481	jj squash --ignore-immutable -R .
482	jj git push --bookmark <bookmark> -R .
483	```
484	
485	If you squash somewhere other than `@-`, add
486	`jj bookmark set <bookmark> -r <target> -R .` between those
487	two commands so the bookmark lands on the rewritten commit.
488	
489	`.claude` is also mutable via this pattern when needed, though
490	push's `finalize-claude` stage normally handles trailing session
491	writes so you rarely hit this case there.
492	
493	### Manual finalize fallback
494	
495	If push exited before `finalize-claude` (e.g. `--no-finalize`
496	was set, or a failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`),
497	run finalize by hand:
498	
499	```
500	vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
501	```
502	
503	**Nothing should happen after finalize** — no memory writes, no
504	tool calls, no additional output. If any work is done after
505	finalize, run finalize again so the trailing writes are captured.
506	Do **not** echo or restate the finalize output — the Bash tool
507	already displays it.
508	
509	**Clear push's saved state after any out-of-band recovery** —
510	manual `vc-x1 finalize`, manual `jj squash --ignore-immutable`
511	+ force-push, etc., all leave `.vc-x1/push-state.toml` pointing
512	at a now-stale halt point. Either `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml`
513	or run `vc-x1 push <bookmark> --restart` (which clears and
514	restarts in one go) before the next `vc-x1 push`. Otherwise
515	push resumes from a bogus stage and can falsely declare
516	success.
517

file.content:
# CLAUDE.md - Bot Instructions

## Project Structure

This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:

1. **App repo** (`/` — project root): Contains the application source code.
2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.

Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.

## Repo Paths (relative from project root)

- App repo: `.` (project root)
- Bot session repo: `.claude`
  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)

## Working Directory

Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
command failures downstream.

When invoking shell commands, prefer the shortest unambiguous path —
usually relative to cwd (`ls notes/`, not
`ls /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/notes/`). Long absolute paths
clutter the transcript without adding information. Out-of-workspace
paths (`/tmp/...`, `~/.config/...`) stay absolute. Read/Edit/Write
tool args also stay absolute — that's a tool-boundary constraint,
not a stylistic choice.

## Memory

Do not use the bot's per-project memory directory
(`~/.claude/projects/<path>/memory/`). In a dual-repo setup with
CLAUDE.md it provides no capability CLAUDE.md doesn't already cover,
and it loses on discoverability:

- **CLAUDE.md** — at the repo root, a well-known location for bot
  instructions, committed, reviewable, visible to every collaborator
  (human or bot).
- **Memory directory** — hidden under the user's home, tied to one
  machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed.

Easy for everyone to find beats convenient for the bot alone. Put
durable context in CLAUDE.md (or committed `notes/`) instead.

## Speculation marker

Durable text the bot writes — CLAUDE.md, `notes/`, commit bodies,
chores sections — should stick to observations and direct descriptions
of the code or data. If a mechanism, hypothesis, or causal claim
enters the text, prefix it with "The bot thinks ..." so a reader can
tell the measured from the inferred.

**Why:** unmarked speculation reads like evidence, and a future reader
(or the bot on a later session) can pick it up as a known fact when
it's not. Measured / inferred is a distinction worth keeping visible
in the written record.

**How to apply:** observations and factual descriptions need no
marker. Prefix with "The bot thinks ..." (or a close variant like
"The bot's guess is ...") when the claim is a mechanism ("X wins
because Y caches better"), a cause ("the drift was due to thermal
state"), a prediction ("this should scale linearly"), or any
reasoning not directly supported by the data on hand.

## Committing

Use `-R` (`--repository`) at the end to target the correct repo. Use
relative paths to reduce noise. Putting `-R` last keeps the verb/action
visible at the start of the command.

### App repo
```
jj commit -m \
"title" \
-m "body

ochid: /.claude/<changeID>" \
-R .
```

### Bot session repo
```
jj commit -m \
"title" \
-m "body

ochid: /<changeID>" \
-R .claude
```

## jj Basics

- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` — push a bookmark (no
  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
  session because session data updates continuously.

## Commit Message Style

Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with
a version suffix:

```
<type>: <short description> (<version>)
```

- **Title**: target ~50 chars, short summary of *what* changed.
  Include the version. Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`,
  `test`, `docs`, `chore`.
- **App-repo body**: short intro paragraph (1–3 sentences), then a
  terse bullet list. Each bullet corresponds one-to-one with the
  edits structure already documented in `notes/chores-*.md` for
  this step — just the file and a one-line gist (e.g.
  `README.md: new Overview intro`). Do *not* restate the detail
  that lives in chores; the commit body is a scan-able index, not
  a duplicate. The chores section is the source of truth.
- **Session-repo body**: terse intro + a few session-activity
  bullets. Doesn't need to mirror chores since it describes
  in-session work, not code changes.
- Examples:
  - `feat: add fix-ochid subcommand (0.22.0)`
  - `fix: fix-ochid prefix bug (0.22.1)`
  - `refactor: deduplicate common CLI flags (0.21.1)`

## Pre-commit Requirements

### User approval

Never execute commit, squash, push, or finalize commands without the
user's explicit approval. Present changes for review first; only run
them after the user confirms. This applies to late changes too —
pause for review before squashing into an existing commit.

### Review before proposing the commit block

After finishing a unit of work, **summarize what changed and stop
there**. Do not pre-emptively lay out the Checkpoint-1 commit
commands. Wait for the user to signal review is complete before
proposing the commit block. Changes during review are the norm,
not the exception; proposing commit text too early creates noise
and signals that I consider the work done when it usually isn't.

This applies per-step in a multi-step flow too — each step gets a
review pause before its commit block appears.

Signals that review is complete include explicit approval ("let's
commit", "looks good, commit it") **and any directive to start the
next step** ("do step 4", "next", "go N+1"). In that case the
previous step must be committed first — always commit the current
step before starting the next; don't ask.

### Notes references

Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
See [Todo format](notes/README.md#todo-format) for details.

### Markdown anchor links

GitHub anchor algorithm: lowercase, strip non-alphanumeric
characters in place, map remaining spaces to hyphens 1-for-1. Do
**not** collapse adjacent whitespace — so `a + b` → `a--b` (spaces
on both sides of `+`), but `a: b` → `a-b` (only trailing space on
`:`). General markdown reference:
[markdownguide.org](https://www.markdownguide.org). GitHub
publishes no official spec for auto-generated anchors; the
de-facto reference implementation is
[github-slugger](https://github.com/Flet/github-slugger).

### Versioning

Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based
on scope:

- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump
  directly to `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
- **Multi-step** (for exploratory/large changes): bump to `X.Y.Z-0`,
  implement across multiple commits incrementing the numeric
  suffix. The final commit drops the suffix.

The plan should recommend one approach and get user approval before
starting.

For multi-step:

1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-0` with the plan and commit as a chore
   marker.
2. Implement in one or more `X.Y.Z-N` commits (increment N as
   needed).
3. Final commit bumps to `X.Y.Z` (no suffix), updates
   `notes/todo.md` and `notes/chores-*.md` — this is the "done"
   marker.

Multi-step cycles surface the ladder at the top of
`notes/todo.md > ## In Progress` as a bullet list with `(done)` /
`(current)` markers — see the file's intro paragraph for the
format. Update the markers as each step ships so the In Progress
view stays current at a glance.

**Why numeric suffixes (`-0`, `-1`, …) rather than `-devN`:**
semver pre-release identifiers may consist of a single numeric
component, and they compare numerically per spec. So
`X.Y.Z-1 < X.Y.Z-2 < … < X.Y.Z` correctly orders the dev ladder
below the done marker. Cargo accepts this form. The `-dev` prefix
adds no information the git log doesn't already convey and
doubles typing per commit.

The final release commit (no suffix) signals completion rather than
amending prior commits. This keeps history readable and makes it easy
to see which commits were exploratory vs final.

### Chores section headers

Chores section headers use trailing version format:

```
## Description (X.Y.Z)
```

Example: `` ## Add `fn claude-symlink` (0.27.0) ``

### Pre-commit checklist

Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:

1. `cargo fmt`
2. `cargo clippy`
3. `cargo test`
4. `cargo install --path . --locked` (if applicable) — `--locked`
   is required: without it, `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock`
   and re-resolves from scratch, which can pick incompatible
   versions even when `cargo build` / `cargo test` succeed.
5. Retest after install
6. Update `notes/todo.md` — add to `## Done` if completing a task
7. Update `notes/chores-*.md` — add a subsection describing the change
8. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new flags,
   new subcommands, changed behavior)

## Code Conventions

### Doc comments on every file, function, and method

Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
comment exists, not that it be long.

This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
module / item surface are expected; inline explanatory comments
inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
non-obvious WHY.

**Shape:** short intro (1–3 sentences, shorter is better), then
a `-` bullet list for any structured details. Avoid long prose
paragraphs — they read as a wall of text and hide the structure
that bullets make scannable. Same shape applies to:

- Module / function / struct / field doc comments in `.rs` files.
- Chore descriptions in `notes/chores-NN.md`.
- Todo entries in `notes/todo.md` (when an item needs more than
  one line of detail; pure one-liners are still fine).

**Clap-derive args:** doc comments on `#[arg(...)]` fields drive
`--help` output. Clap reflows by default and collapses bullets
into running prose. Add `#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, ...)]` on
any field whose doc comment uses bullets so each `- …` lands on
its own line in the rendered help.

### `// OK: …` comments on `unwrap*` calls (Rust)

Non-test code that calls `.unwrap()`, `.unwrap_or(…)`,
`.unwrap_or_default()`, or `.unwrap_or_else(…)` must have a trailing
`// OK: …` comment that justifies why the call is acceptable.

- `// OK: <specific reason>` — document the real precondition,
  invariant, or domain reason. Preferred whenever the reason isn't
  self-evident.
- `// OK: obvious` — the default is self-evident from context (e.g.
  `desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("")` — empty desc → empty title).

Bare `// OK` is not used (reads like a truncated comment).
Abbreviations (e.g. `SE`) are not used because they require a decoder
ring for readers seeing the code out of context.

For provably-unreachable `.unwrap()` calls, also prefix with
`#[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]` so the site stays silent if we enable
the project-wide `clippy::unwrap_used` lint later.

```rust
let max = stderr_level.unwrap_or(LevelFilter::Info); // OK: default verbosity when -v/-vv absent
let first_line = desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("");  // OK: obvious

match matches.len() {
    1 => {
        #[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]
        // OK: `1 =>` arm guarantees matches.len() == 1
        Ok(TitleMatch::One(matches.into_iter().next().unwrap()))
    }
    // ...
}
```

Tests (`#[cfg(test)]`) are exempt — panicking on setup failure is the
correct test behavior.

### Ask for clarification on ambiguous input

When user input is ambiguous or missing necessary detail, stop and
ask a specific question. Do not proceed on a guess and hope the
result lands right — a clarifying question costs a few seconds;
redoing misaligned work costs much more.

### Recognize when stuck

If a simple task has eaten 5+ minutes of thinking or back-and-forth
without progress, stop. Summarize what's blocking — unclear
requirements, unfamiliar API surface, conflicting signals — and ask.
Continued flailing produces worse outcomes than a direct "I'm stuck
on X."

## ochid Trailers

Every commit body must include an `ochid:` trailer pointing to the
counterpart commit in the other repo. The value is a workspace-root-relative
path followed by the changeID:

- App repo commits point to `.claude`: `ochid: /.claude/<changeID>`
- Bot session commits point to app repo: `ochid: /<changeID>`

Use `vc-x1 chid -R .,.claude -L` to get both changeIDs (first line
is app repo, second is `.claude`).

## Commit-Push-Finalize Flow

**Use `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` — it wraps the full flow in one
command with two interactive approval gates.** Push runs
preflight (sync + fmt + clippy + test), prompts for review,
composes the commit message (via `--title`/`--body` or `$EDITOR`),
prompts for message approval, then commits both repos → advances
bookmarks → pushes app → finalizes `.claude`. Failures inside the
local mutation window roll both repos back via `jj op restore`;
after `push-app` succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
recovery is forward-only.

**Run this flow after every step** — not only at session end.
Single-step and multi-step changes are of equal importance: a
single-step change is one `push` invocation; a multi-step change
is one `push` per `X.Y.Z-N` commit plus one for the final release
commit. Each step gets its own commit, its own push, and its own
finalize — so dev markers land on the remote and in `.claude` as
they happen rather than being batched until the end.

### Run `vc-x1 push`

```
vc-x1 push main                                      # interactive (review + $EDITOR)
vc-x1 push main --title "..." --body "..."           # flags skip $EDITOR
vc-x1 push main --yes --title "..." --body "..."     # full non-interactive
vc-x1 push main --dry-run                            # preview (no side effects)
vc-x1 push main --from commit-app                    # resume from specific stage
vc-x1 push --status                                  # show saved state
vc-x1 push main --restart                            # clear saved state; start fresh
```

The two approval gates are surfaced by push itself:

1. **Review** — push prints `jj diff --stat` for both repos and
   prompts `[y/N]`. Approve = "the work is done right".
2. **Message** — push either uses `--title`/`--body` (non-editor
   path) or opens `$EDITOR` on a template. Approve = "the message
   reads right".

Both titles and bodies are the **same** across the two commits;
only the `ochid:` trailer differs per repo. Push collects the
pre-commit chids internally so you don't hand-manage them.

For the full flag list and stage machine, see `vc-x1 push --help`
and `notes/chores-05.md > Add push subcommand (0.37.0)`.

### Bot communication during the flow

When applying the flow on the user's behalf, use plain prose at
each gate — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal", "Checkpoint N",
etc.):

1. **After completing the work** — summarize what changed
   (file-by-file or feature-by-feature, terse) and end with
   something like:

   > Work complete. Please review. On approval I'll prep the
   > commit title and body for a second approval before pushing.

2. **After review approval** — present the proposed `$TITLE` and
   `$BODY` explicitly, then ask permission to run `vc-x1 push`.
   Do **not** spell out the full
   `vc-x1 push <bookmark> --yes --title "$TITLE" --body "$BODY"`
   invocation by default — it's mechanical and obvious from the
   title/body. Show it only on explicit request (verbose mode /
   debugging).

3. **After execution approval** — run the push command. `push`
   handles commit + bookmark + push + finalize internally;
   nothing should be output after the push command (finalize is
   detached and absolute-last; see "After finalize: stop and
   wait" below).

### Pre-step: `vc-x1 sync` (still useful)

Push's preflight runs `vc-x1 sync --check` as its first step. Check
mode verifies divergence without applying — if anything is `behind`
or `diverged`, preflight errors and you resolve explicitly with
`vc-x1 sync --no-check` before re-running push. This keeps the
two approval gates (review, message) authoritative; push never
performs an unsupervised rebase.

Running sync manually before you *start* editing is still cheap
(one line when clean) and surfaces remote changes earlier:

```
vc-x1 sync --check                    # dual-repo workspace, verify
vc-x1 sync --no-check                 # apply (rebase / fast-forward)
vc-x1 sync --check -R .               # single-repo project
vc-x1 sync --check --quiet            # silent; exit code signals result
```

Bots and scripts must pass `--check` or `--no-check` explicitly —
defaults can shift, explicit flags lock in the contract. Interactive
use can take the default (which is `--check`).

Output shape:

- **Clean**: one line — `sync: N repos, all bookmarks up-to-date`.
  Note: scope is bookmark-vs-remote tracking, not working-copy
  cleanliness — `@` may have uncommitted changes; sync doesn't
  speak to that. Proceed.
- **Action needed** (`behind` / `diverged`) under `--check`:
  per-repo fetch + state lines, then a fatal
  `sync: N repos need action — resolve with vc-x1 sync --no-check
  and re-run`. Inspect, run `vc-x1 sync --no-check`, then proceed.
- **`--quiet`**: no output; exit code is the only signal.

### After finalize: stop and wait

After `vc-x1 finalize` is launched — **whether mid-session per-step
or at session end** — you **MUST NEVER** proceed to a next step, edit
files, run tools, or emit any text (prose, recaps, acknowledgements),
until the user explicitly directs you to continue. Treat finalize as
a hard stop for the whole turn. Any final words (e.g. "next is ...")
must be said in the approval prompt *before* executing finalize; the
finalize `Bash` call is the last thing in the turn and nothing
follows it.

This holds even when the next step seems obvious (e.g. "next is
N+1" or "now I should bump the version and commit the release").
Wait. The user controls cadence — every push+finalize is a checkpoint
they may want to inspect, think about, hand off, or take a break at.
Auto-proceeding bypasses that checkpoint and produces unwanted writes
between finalize and the next explicit instruction.

Exceptions to this rule may emerge later but are not authorized at
this stage. Until told otherwise, treat as absolute.

### Late changes after push

If the app repo needs a tweak after `push-app` succeeded (e.g.
updating CLAUDE.md or memory), the commit is immutable. Use
`--ignore-immutable` to squash the changes into `@-` — the
bookmark moves with the rewritten commit — then re-push:

```
jj squash --ignore-immutable -R .
jj git push --bookmark <bookmark> -R .
```

If you squash somewhere other than `@-`, add
`jj bookmark set <bookmark> -r <target> -R .` between those
two commands so the bookmark lands on the rewritten commit.

`.claude` is also mutable via this pattern when needed, though
push's `finalize-claude` stage normally handles trailing session
writes so you rarely hit this case there.

### Manual finalize fallback

If push exited before `finalize-claude` (e.g. `--no-finalize`
was set, or a failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`),
run finalize by hand:

```
vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
```

**Nothing should happen after finalize** — no memory writes, no
tool calls, no additional output. If any work is done after
finalize, run finalize again so the trailing writes are captured.
Do **not** echo or restate the finalize output — the Bash tool
already displays it.

**Clear push's saved state after any out-of-band recovery** —
manual `vc-x1 finalize`, manual `jj squash --ignore-immutable`
+ force-push, etc., all leave `.vc-x1/push-state.toml` pointing
at a now-stale halt point. Either `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml`
or run `vc-x1 push <bookmark> --restart` (which clears and
restarts in one go) before the next `vc-x1 push`. Otherwise
push resumes from a bogus stage and can falsely declare
success.

jj describe -m "docs: CLAUDE.md substep-protocol pointer" -m "Adds a #### Substeps subsection under ### Versioning that
points to notes/substep-protocol.md and notes/jj-revsets.md.
This is the minimal integration — the formal protocol body
lives in notes/, CLAUDE.md just announces it.

Conservative scope by design. The user's broader \"make
CLAUDE.md better organized\" ask wants iteration, not a
solo restructure. Reorg candidates surfaced for next-session
discussion (not committed):

- ## Committing (lines 69-93) shows raw jj commit patterns
  as if canonical, but the canonical workflow is vc-x1 push
  per ## Commit-Push-Finalize Flow. The raw examples are
  still useful as low-level mechanics; could be reframed as
  \"reference: what push does under the hood\" or moved
  later in the file.
- ## Committing, ## Commit Message Style, and ## ochid
  Trailers are three sections that all speak to commit
  shape. They could be consolidated into one ## Commits
  section with subsections.
- ## Commit-Push-Finalize Flow has six subsections — the
  two recovery ones (### Late changes after push, ### Manual
  finalize fallback) could fork into their own ## Recovery

content.text:
All four substeps shipped. Here's the final state:

```
@  pynknoqu  docs: CLAUDE.md substep-protocol pointer        (M CLAUDE.md)
○  xqpnxtyu  docs: substep-protocol.md formal protocol       (M notes/substep-protocol.md)
○  lzqmlpvy  docs: jj-revsets.md review fixes                (M notes/jj-revsets.md)
○  wmmxkuoz  chore: scratch — jj-revsets learning ...        (A jj-revsets/substep-protocol/substep-test)
                  ↑ bookmark substep-protocol-x1
◆  lulqxovr  docs: capture 0.41.1 init+clone redesign ...    (main, immutable)
```

**What landed:**

- **Substep 1** (`notes/jj-revsets.md`): typos and incomplete sentences fixed (`usde`/`revsions`/`be create`/`Summanry`/`it's`/cid `ab→ad` mismatch); missing closing quotes on `'root()'` and `'roots(~root())'`; two `### Interpretation` blocks added — one explaining `@-`/`@+` vs `@..`/`@::`/`..@`/`::@` (open vs closed range, inclusion of root/heads), one explaining chid prefix resolution and the `w+` worked example.

- **Substep 2** (no commit — folded into substep 3): validated close-out squash recipes against fresh ladders from `notes/substep-test.sh`. The validated recipe is `jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .` — squashes substep diffs *upward* into `@`, leaves linear `<base> → @`, intermediate commits auto-abandoned, `@`'s description preserved. Tested at N=3 and N=5. The draft's `--into base` variant works mechanically but leaves an empty `@` and forces `jj edit @-` onto `base`. The `jj op restore` alternative was misleading — it discards substep work, so it's recovery, not close-out.

- **Substep 3** (`notes/substep-protocol.md`): rewritten as a standalone formal protocol — purpose, when-to-use, per-substep contract (`cargo test --bins` non-negotiable), navigation primitives (cross-link to `jj-revsets.md`), validated close-out recipe, recovery (3 modes), worked end-to-end example. Dropped the "draft text — proposed addition under" framing and the working-hypothesis hedges.

- **Substep 4** (`CLAUDE.md`): added a `#### Substeps within a multi-step X.Y.Z-N` subsection under `### Versioning` pointing at `notes/substep-protocol.md` and `notes/jj-revsets.md`. Conservative scope.

**Reorg candidates I noticed but didn't commit** (left in substep 4's commit body for next-session discussion):
- `## Committing` shows raw `jj commit` as if canonical, but `vc-x1 push` is the canonical workflow per `## Commit-Push-Finalize Flow`. Could be reframed as "reference" or moved later.
- `## Committing` + `## Commit Message Style` + `## ochid Trailers` all speak to commit shape — could consolidate into one `## Commits` section.
- `### Late changes after push` and `### Manual finalize fallback` could fork into a `## Recovery` to surface them.
- File is 500+ lines now; a top-of-file TOC would help.

**Loose ends and questions for when you're back:**

1. **Versioning at close-out.** This branch is notes/CLAUDE.md only — no code change. The 0.42.0 cycle is in flight on `main`. Options: (a) version this as `0.42.0-3.5` or `0.42.0-3-substep` (interleaves with cycle), (b) version as `0.42.1` (independent patch line), (c) fold into the next cycle's chores entry without its own version. Your call.

2. **Close-out recipe to run when you approve.** The first substep's parent is `main`, so the recipe collapsing the whole branch is:
   ```
   jj squash --from "main..@-" --into @ -u -R .
   ```
   That'll squash {scratch-base, jj-revsets-fixes, formal-protocol, CLAUDE.md-pointer} → single `@` on `main`, ready for `vc-x1 push main --title "..." --body "..."`.

3. **`sc.txt` / `z.txt`.** Vanished sometime before substep 1's `jj st`. They were uncommitted scratch at session start; the recipe in `sc.txt` and the captured log in `z.txt` are now fully encoded into `notes/substep-protocol.md`, so no content lost — but I can't account for the disappearance and `jj op log` doesn't show me deleting them. Worth noting since you may have expected them in the scratch base commit.

4. **`init-clone-refactor` branch** still has its conflicted state (`0.41.1-6.0` through `-6.5`). Untouched, out of scope here.

Ladder is sitting on `substep-protocol-x1` for your review.

1	Learning about revsets to develop a mental model of jj
2	and I used notes/substep-test.sh written by the bot to
3	create the repo here.
4	
5	To develop a mental model look at the examples and
6	develop explanations for them. 
7	
8	The basic information is that revsets, set of revisions,
9	are used to address commits. A revision can be identified
10	using "@", chid or cid and revsets can be created using
11	those identifiers plus operators "-", "+", ".." and "::".
12	There is a complete language available, see
13	`jj help -k revsets` for more information.
14	
15	Summary:
16	 - A revision (rev) used for addressing/referring to commits
17	 - jj support a change_id (chid) and commit_id (cid)
18	   - The chid is permanent and doesn't change
19	   - the cid is the sha of the current commit and
20	     changes with any change to it or its ancestors
21	 - A revset is a set of revisions
22	 - In many jj commands revsets can be use to address multiple commits
23	 - There is one root commit; chid=zzz sha=0 owner=root
24	 - The root commit never has content nor description
25	 - There can be multiple independent lineages of commits off root()
26	   - to create:
27	     - `jj new 'root()'`
28	     - `git switch --orphan <name>`
29	   - to see these:
30	     - `jj log -r 'roots(~root())'`
31	     - `git log --all --max-parents=0 --oneline`
32	
33	The examples below show these primitives in action.
34	
35	## A jj repo with some commits
36	
37	Some examples of revisions/rev:
38	   - @, chid=tkpvsrop, cid=ad2ba9b4 is the current commit
39	   - @-, chid=wxtmosqz, cid=35ac2422 is the ancestor of the current commit
40	   - @--, chid=vktlnyvm, cid=a0b0285e is the ancestor of the ancestor and so on
41	
42	```
43	wink@3900x 26-05-01T16:44:41.913Z:/tmp/substep-test (@tkpvsrop)
44	$ jj log -r 'all()'
45	@  tkpvsrop wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 ad2ba9b4
46	│  count 3
47	○  wxtmosqz wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 35ac2422
48	│  count 2
49	○  vktlnyvm wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 a0b0285e
50	│  count 1
51	○  nvwytkrw wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 base 54fd18f7
52	│  count 0
53	◆  zzzzzzzz root() 00000000
54	wink@3900x 26-05-01T16:45:21.527Z:/tmp/substep-test (@tkpvsrop)
55	```
56	
57	Move @ to vktlnyvm. (This could also have been done with `jj edit -r @--`.)
58	
59	```
60	wink@3900x 26-05-01T16:45:21.527Z:/tmp/substep-test (@tkpvsrop)

99	│  count 2
100	~

113	│  count 3

120	│  count 3
121	○  wxtmosqz wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 35ac2422
122	│  count 2
123	@  vktlnyvm wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 a0b0285e
124	│  count 1
125	~
126	wink@3900x 26-05-01T17:18:22.096Z:/tmp/substep-test (@vktlnyvm)
127	$ jj log -r ..@
128	@  vktlnyvm wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 a0b0285e
129	│  count 1
130	○  nvwytkrw wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 base 54fd18f7
131	│  count 0
132	~
133	wink@3900x 26-05-01T17:18:50.526Z:/tmp/substep-test (@vktlnyvm)
134	$ jj log -r ::@
135	@  vktlnyvm wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 a0b0285e
136	│  count 1
137	○  nvwytkrw wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 base 54fd18f7
138	│  count 0
139	◆  zzzzzzzz root() 00000000
140	wink@3900x 26-05-01T17:19:07.714Z:/tmp/substep-test (@vktlnyvm)
141	```
142	
143	### Interpretation
144	
145	- `@-` and `@--` resolve to single revisions: parent and
146	  grandparent of @. `@+` and `@++` are children. The
147	  blank output for `@+++` and `@---` is the empty revset
148	  — there is no revision that far away in this chain.
149	- `@..` and `@::` are ranges going outward from @ toward
150	  descendants:
151	  - `@..` is the *open* form: descendants of @, **excluding**
152	    @ itself (here: count 2 and count 3).
153	  - `@::` is the *closed* form: descendants of @, **including**
154	    @ itself (here: @=count 1, count 2, count 3).
155	- `..@` and `::@` are ranges going inward from some implicit
156	  start toward ancestors of @:
157	  - `..@` includes @ and its ancestors but **excludes** the
158	    root commit (here: @=count 1, base=count 0).
159	  - `::@` includes the root commit as well (here: @=count 1,
160	    base=count 0, root).
161	
162	Mnemonic: `..` and `::` both produce ranges; `::` includes
163	the implicit endpoint (root or visible heads), `..` excludes
164	it. The named operand is always part of the result on the
165	target side; on the source side it depends on which dot-form
166	is used (excluded by `..`, included by `::`).
167	
168	## Example of referencing commits with absolute revsets
169	
170	```
171	wink@3900x 26-05-01T17:19:07.714Z:/tmp/substep-test (@vktlnyvm)
172	$ jj log -r v
173	@  vktlnyvm wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 a0b0285e
174	│  count 1
175	~
176	wink@3900x 26-05-01T17:24:10.385Z:/tmp/substep-test (@vktlnyvm)
177	$ jj log -r vktln
178	@  vktlnyvm wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 a0b0285e
179	│  count 1
180	~
181	wink@3900x 26-05-01T17:24:26.707Z:/tmp/substep-test (@vktlnyvm)
182	$ jj log -r w+
183	○  tkpvsrop wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 ad2ba9b4
184	│  count 3
185	~
186	wink@3900x 26-05-01T17:25:10.856Z:/tmp/substep-test (@vktlnyvm)
187	$ jj log -r w--
188	○  nvwytkrw wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 08:41:05 base 54fd18f7

190	~
191	wink@3900x 26-05-01T17:25:22.852Z:/tmp/substep-test (@vktlnyvm)
